title: Billys 'Square Affair'
content:
آ آ آ آ Long Bill, the captain of the push, was tired of his estate,
آ آ آ آ And wished to change his life and win the love of something ï؟½straightï؟½;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas rumourï؟½d that the Gory B.ï؟½s had heard Long Bill declare
آ آ آ آ That he would turn respectable and wed a ï؟½square affair.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He craved the kiss of innocence; his spirit longed to rise;
آ آ آ آ The ï؟½Crimson Streak,ï؟½ his faithful ï؟½piece,ï؟½ grew hateful in his eyes;
آ آ آ آ (And though, in her entirety, the Crimson Streak ï؟½was there,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ I grieve to state the Crimson Streak was not a ï؟½square affair.ï؟½)

آ آ آ آ He wanted clothes, a masher suit, he wanted boots and hat;
آ آ آ آ His girl had earned a quid or two, he wouldnï؟½t part with that;
آ آ آ آ And so he went to Brickfield Hill, and from a draper there
آ آ آ آ He ï؟½shookï؟½ the proper kind of togs to fetch a ï؟½square affair.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Long Bill went to the barberï؟½s shop and had a shave and singe,
آ آ آ آ And from his narrow forehead combed his darling Mabel fringe;
آ آ آ آ Long Bill put on a ï؟½square cutï؟½ and he brushed his boots with care,
آ آ آ آ And roved about the Gardens till he mashed a ï؟½square affair.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ She was a tony servant-girl from somewhere on ï؟½the Shore;ï؟½
آ آ آ آ She dressed in style that suited Bill, he could not wish for more.
آ آ آ آ While in her guileless presence he had ceased to chew or swear,
آ آ آ آ He knew the kind of barrack that can fetch a square affair.

آ آ آ آ To thus desert his donah old was risky and a sin,
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½twould have served him right if she had caved his garret in.
آ آ آ آ The Gory Bleeders thought it too, and warned him to take care
آ آ آ آ In case the Crimson Streak got scent of Billyï؟½s square affair.

آ آ آ آ He took her to the stalls; ï؟½twas dear, but Billy said ï؟½Wot odds!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ He couldnï؟½t take his square affair amongst the crimson gods.
آ آ آ آ They wandered in the park at night, and hugged each other there, 
آ آ آ آ But, ah! the Crimson Streak got wind of Billyï؟½s square affair!

آ آ آ آ ï؟½The blank and space and stars!ï؟½ she yelled; ï؟½the nameless crimson dash!
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Iï؟½ll smash the blanky crimson and his square affair, Iï؟½ll smashï؟½, 
آ آ آ آ In short, she drank and raved and shrieked and tore her crimson hair,
آ آ آ آ And swore to murder Billy and to pound his square affair.

آ آ آ آ And so one summer evening, as the day was growing dim,
آ آ آ آ She watched her bloke go out, and foxed his square affair and him.
آ آ آ آ That night the park was startled by the shrieks that rent the air, 
آ آ آ آ The ï؟½Streakï؟½ had gone for Billy and for Billyï؟½s square affair.

آ آ آ آ The ï؟½goryï؟½ push had foxed the Streak, they foxed her to the park,
آ آ آ آ And they, of course; were close at hand to see the bleedinï؟½ lark;
آ آ آ آ A cop arrived in time to hear a ï؟½gory B.ï؟½ declare
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Gor blar-me! hereï؟½s the Red Streak foul of Billyï؟½s square affair.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Now Billy scowls about the Rocks, his manly beauty marrï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And Billyï؟½s girl, upon her ï؟½ed, is doinï؟½ six months ï؟½ard;
آ آ آ آ Billï؟½s swivel eye is in a sling, his heart is in despair,
آ آ آ آ And in the Sydney ï؟½Orspital lies Billyï؟½s square affair.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
